APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper tackles automatic poetic verse generation using neural LSTM models and a constrained WFSM-based decoder, a topic squarely within NLP/ML conference scope and of interest to the community working on controllable generation and representation learning. The methodology and evaluations (phonetic encoding, iambic-stress metrics, human judgments, and comparisons to unconstrained and human baselines) match standards expected at top venues, and hyperparameters are reported. Minor concerns for reproducibility and impact remain — code is not mentioned and key results rely on complex transliteration/phonetic preprocessing with known error modes — but these do not outweigh its overall relevance and audience fit. | B(5): This paper sits squarely within NLP/ML concerns: it proposes novel modeling approaches (phonetic-level LSTM and a constrained generation pipeline combining a neural model with a weighted finite-state machine) targeting a linguistically rich creative task. The use of standard datasets (sonnets, broader poetry corpora), quantitative metrics for prosodic conformity, and a large-scale human evaluation that demonstrates competitive human-likeness show it is addressed to the right audience and meets methodological and evaluation expectations of a top venue. Its explicit handling of phonetic encoding, stress patterns and form constraints will be of clear interest to both computational-linguistics and generative-model communities.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper tackles automatic poetic verse generation using neural LSTM models and a constrained WFSM-based decoder, a topic squarely within NLP/ML conference scope and of interest to the community working on controllable generation and representation learning. The methodology and evaluations (phonetic encoding, iambic-stress metrics, human judgments, and comparisons to unconstrained and human baselines) match standards expected at top venues, and hyperparameters are reported. Minor concerns for reproducibility and impact remain — code is not mentioned and key results rely on complex transliteration/phonetic preprocessing with known error modes — but these do not outweigh its overall relevance and audience fit.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper sits squarely within NLP/ML concerns: it proposes novel modeling approaches (phonetic-level LSTM and a constrained generation pipeline combining a neural model with a weighted finite-state machine) targeting a linguistically rich creative task. The use of standard datasets (sonnets, broader poetry corpora), quantitative metrics for prosodic conformity, and a large-scale human evaluation that demonstrates competitive human-likeness show it is addressed to the right audience and meets methodological and evaluation expectations of a top venue. Its explicit handling of phonetic encoding, stress patterns and form constraints will be of clear interest to both computational-linguistics and generative-model communities.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper tackles a clear and engaging problem—automatic generation of metrically and rhymically constrained poetry—using two complementary technical approaches (phonetic-level LSTM and constrained character-level generation with weighted FSA) and reports human evaluations where machine poems were judged human 54% of the time. The constrained-generation technique that separates content modeling from form enforcement is likely to influence how researchers design controlled generation systems, and the successful phonetic modeling suggests a viable path for incorporating linguistic form into sequence models. Limitations noted (transliteration/pronunciation dictionary issues, low reconstruction accuracy on noisy corpora) reduce immediate reproducibility and deployment, but the methods and large-scale human evaluation open useful follow-up directions for form-constrained text generation beyond poetry. | B(4): This paper addresses an important and underexplored problem—learning poetic form and generating metrically and rhymically constrained verse—using two complementary neural approaches that convincingly capture phonetic devices (rhyme, rhythm, alliteration) and produce poems judged human 54% of the time. The combination of a phonetic-level LSTM and a discriminative WFSM constraint framework is likely to change how researchers approach form/content separation in creative language generation and to enable practical systems for arbitrary poetic forms. While transliteration and dictionary limitations constrain immediate robustness, the methods open clear new research directions (morpheme-level models, better phonetic preprocessing) and have broader applicability for digital humanities and creative-assistant tools.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper tackles a clear and engaging problem—automatic generation of metrically and rhymically constrained poetry—using two complementary technical approaches (phonetic-level LSTM and constrained character-level generation with weighted FSA) and reports human evaluations where machine poems were judged human 54% of the time. The constrained-generation technique that separates content modeling from form enforcement is likely to influence how researchers design controlled generation systems, and the successful phonetic modeling suggests a viable path for incorporating linguistic form into sequence models. Limitations noted (transliteration/pronunciation dictionary issues, low reconstruction accuracy on noisy corpora) reduce immediate reproducibility and deployment, but the methods and large-scale human evaluation open useful follow-up directions for form-constrained text generation beyond poetry.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses an important and underexplored problem—learning poetic form and generating metrically and rhymically constrained verse—using two complementary neural approaches that convincingly capture phonetic devices (rhyme, rhythm, alliteration) and produce poems judged human 54% of the time. The combination of a phonetic-level LSTM and a discriminative WFSM constraint framework is likely to change how researchers approach form/content separation in creative language generation and to enable practical systems for arbitrary poetic forms. While transliteration and dictionary limitations constrain immediate robustness, the methods open clear new research directions (morpheme-level models, better phonetic preprocessing) and have broader applicability for digital humanities and creative-assistant tools.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper proposes two distinct methodological contributions — an LSTM trained on phonetic encodings (leveraging CMU pronunciations plus letter-to-sound transliteration) and a constrained generation scheme that combines a character-level generative model with a discriminative weighted finite-state machine to enforce poetic form. These are clear, non-trivial extensions beyond vanilla character/word models used previously and are differentiated from prior work in the summary. While elements (phonetic encodings, constrained decoding) reuse known techniques, their integration for rhythmic poetry generation and explicit form enforcement would be informative to experts and represents notable novelty rather than merely incremental work. | B(4): The paper presents a notable, well-differentiated pair of methods: training an LSTM on a phonetic encoding to learn rhyme/rhythm implicitly, and a constrained generation setup that couples a generative character model with a discriminative weighted finite-state machine to enforce form. Both approaches go beyond straightforward character-level or dictionary-based systems by explicitly addressing phonetics and hard-form constraints, which is a meaningful extension of current neural poetry work. Given the combination of phonetic-level modeling and the WFSA-based constraint mechanism, the contribution would likely surprise practitioners familiar with prior unconstrained neural poem generators, though it is not a radical theoretical departure from existing sequence modeling and constraint-enforcement techniques.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes two distinct methodological contributions — an LSTM trained on phonetic encodings (leveraging CMU pronunciations plus letter-to-sound transliteration) and a constrained generation scheme that combines a character-level generative model with a discriminative weighted finite-state machine to enforce poetic form. These are clear, non-trivial extensions beyond vanilla character/word models used previously and are differentiated from prior work in the summary. While elements (phonetic encodings, constrained decoding) reuse known techniques, their integration for rhythmic poetry generation and explicit form enforcement would be informative to experts and represents notable novelty rather than merely incremental work.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a notable, well-differentiated pair of methods: training an LSTM on a phonetic encoding to learn rhyme/rhythm implicitly, and a constrained generation setup that couples a generative character model with a discriminative weighted finite-state machine to enforce form. Both approaches go beyond straightforward character-level or dictionary-based systems by explicitly addressing phonetics and hard-form constraints, which is a meaningful extension of current neural poetry work. Given the combination of phonetic-level modeling and the WFSA-based constraint mechanism, the contribution would likely surprise practitioners familiar with prior unconstrained neural poem generators, though it is not a radical theoretical departure from existing sequence modeling and constraint-enforcement techniques.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper evaluates both proposed methods against reasonable baselines (an unconstrained character model and human-generated poetry) using shared datasets and multiple metrics (syllabic-stress mistakes, trigram similarity, human judgments), which supports fair empirical comparison. However, it appears to omit comparisons to other specialized constrained-generation or prior rule-based poetry systems and does not report whether prior automatic methods sometimes match or exceed their models on the same metrics, so some important baselines and explicit acknowledgment of comparable prior performance are missing. The authors do analyze failure modes (transliteration/pronunciation-dictionary errors) and report hyperparameters, which aids reproducibility, but a more comprehensive set of competing automatic methods would be needed for a fully exhaustive comparison. | B(3): The paper compares its two proposed approaches against an unconstrained character model and human-written poetry and uses both quantitative metrics (stress-mistake rates, trigram similarity) and a large-scale human evaluation, which supports some fair head-to-head assessment. However, only a minimal set of baselines is reported (no comparison to other prior constrained-generation or phonetic/rule-based poetic systems), so important competing approaches appear missing despite the claim of novelty. The authors do acknowledge limitations where prior resources (pronunciation dictionaries, transliteration) affect performance, but the analysis falls short of situating results against the strongest relevant prior work. From my perspective as a literary-NLP scholar, this yields useful but incomplete empirical comparison.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates both proposed methods against reasonable baselines (an unconstrained character model and human-generated poetry) using shared datasets and multiple metrics (syllabic-stress mistakes, trigram similarity, human judgments), which supports fair empirical comparison. However, it appears to omit comparisons to other specialized constrained-generation or prior rule-based poetry systems and does not report whether prior automatic methods sometimes match or exceed their models on the same metrics, so some important baselines and explicit acknowledgment of comparable prior performance are missing. The authors do analyze failure modes (transliteration/pronunciation-dictionary errors) and report hyperparameters, which aids reproducibility, but a more comprehensive set of competing automatic methods would be needed for a fully exhaustive comparison.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares its two proposed approaches against an unconstrained character model and human-written poetry and uses both quantitative metrics (stress-mistake rates, trigram similarity) and a large-scale human evaluation, which supports some fair head-to-head assessment. However, only a minimal set of baselines is reported (no comparison to other prior constrained-generation or phonetic/rule-based poetic systems), so important competing approaches appear missing despite the claim of novelty. The authors do acknowledge limitations where prior resources (pronunciation dictionaries, transliteration) affect performance, but the analysis falls short of situating results against the strongest relevant prior work. From my perspective as a literary-NLP scholar, this yields useful but incomplete empirical comparison.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper presents measurable experiments (automatic metrics and a large-scale human evaluation) supporting its claims that phonetic-level and constrained character-level approaches can produce rhythmic verse, but important experimental details and controls are missing. Mathematical aspects are standard neural/LFSM methods and require no new proofs; however, reported evidence is weakened by acknowledged transliteration/pronunciation-dictionary errors and lack of code or reproducibility details. The experimental methodology uses reasonable baselines (unconstrained model, human) but lacks clarity on statistical testing, inter-annotator agreement, and some ablations (e.g., impact of transliteration quality), leaving logical gaps. Overall the conclusions are directionally supported but require additional ablation, error analysis, and rigorous statistical reporting to be fully convincing. | B(4): The paper's principal claims are generally supported by the reported experiments: the phonetic-level LSTM shows learning of rhyme/rhythm/alliteration and the constrained character-level + WFSM approach achieves high conformity (70% of lines with zero syllabic-stress mistakes) and favorable human judgments (54% judged as human, one machine poem rated best). Mathematical content is minimal and internally consistent. However, there are technical weaknesses tied to the phonetic preprocessing (noted transliteration and pronunciation-dictionary failures, low per-word reconstruction on Wikipedia) that plausibly explain some errors, and the summary lacks detail on statistical testing and some experimental controls to fully rule out confounds. Given these minor but relevant methodological gaps, the work is technically sound overall but not flawless.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents measurable experiments (automatic metrics and a large-scale human evaluation) supporting its claims that phonetic-level and constrained character-level approaches can produce rhythmic verse, but important experimental details and controls are missing. Mathematical aspects are standard neural/LFSM methods and require no new proofs; however, reported evidence is weakened by acknowledged transliteration/pronunciation-dictionary errors and lack of code or reproducibility details. The experimental methodology uses reasonable baselines (unconstrained model, human) but lacks clarity on statistical testing, inter-annotator agreement, and some ablations (e.g., impact of transliteration quality), leaving logical gaps. Overall the conclusions are directionally supported but require additional ablation, error analysis, and rigorous statistical reporting to be fully convincing.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's principal claims are generally supported by the reported experiments: the phonetic-level LSTM shows learning of rhyme/rhythm/alliteration and the constrained character-level + WFSM approach achieves high conformity (70% of lines with zero syllabic-stress mistakes) and favorable human judgments (54% judged as human, one machine poem rated best). Mathematical content is minimal and internally consistent. However, there are technical weaknesses tied to the phonetic preprocessing (noted transliteration and pronunciation-dictionary failures, low per-word reconstruction on Wikipedia) that plausibly explain some errors, and the summary lacks detail on statistical testing and some experimental controls to fully rule out confounds. Given these minor but relevant methodological gaps, the work is technically sound overall but not flawless.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper presents reasonable empirical evidence (intrinsic metrics on stress/rhyme and a large-scale human evaluation with 54% perceived-as-human and best-rated poem) and reports hyperparameters, but it lacks key ablations and sensitivity analyses that would support causal claims about model components (e.g., role of phonetic encoding vs. architecture choices, or impact of the WFSA constraints). Failure modes and limitations are acknowledged (transliteration/pronunciation-dictionary errors, low per-word reconstruction on Wikipedia), but reproducibility is weak because code/data processing (phonetic transliteration/HMM/ngram) and some implementation details are not provided. Overall the experiments are adequate to demonstrate promise but miss important analyses and release details needed for strong, reproducible claims. | B(4): The paper presents substantial empirical evidence—including intrinsic metrics (70% lines with zero stress mistakes, syllabic-misclassification counts), a large-scale human evaluation (54% of machine poems judged human and a machine poem rated best), and comparisons between a phonetic-level model and a constrained character-level approach—supporting its core claims. It also acknowledges key limitations (transliteration/pronunciation-dictionary errors, low per-word reconstruction on Wikipedia) and reports hyperparameters, and related work coverage is adequate. Missing, however, are explicit ablation or sensitivity analyses isolating contributions of major components (e.g., impact of phonetic encoding vs. model size or constraint weighting), and code availability or fuller reproducibility artifacts are not mentioned, leaving some gaps for complete replication.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents reasonable empirical evidence (intrinsic metrics on stress/rhyme and a large-scale human evaluation with 54% perceived-as-human and best-rated poem) and reports hyperparameters, but it lacks key ablations and sensitivity analyses that would support causal claims about model components (e.g., role of phonetic encoding vs. architecture choices, or impact of the WFSA constraints). Failure modes and limitations are acknowledged (transliteration/pronunciation-dictionary errors, low per-word reconstruction on Wikipedia), but reproducibility is weak because code/data processing (phonetic transliteration/HMM/ngram) and some implementation details are not provided. Overall the experiments are adequate to demonstrate promise but miss important analyses and release details needed for strong, reproducible claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents substantial empirical evidence—including intrinsic metrics (70% lines with zero stress mistakes, syllabic-misclassification counts), a large-scale human evaluation (54% of machine poems judged human and a machine poem rated best), and comparisons between a phonetic-level model and a constrained character-level approach—supporting its core claims. It also acknowledges key limitations (transliteration/pronunciation-dictionary errors, low per-word reconstruction on Wikipedia) and reports hyperparameters, and related work coverage is adequate. Missing, however, are explicit ablation or sensitivity analyses isolating contributions of major components (e.g., impact of phonetic encoding vs. model size or constraint weighting), and code availability or fuller reproducibility artifacts are not mentioned, leaving some gaps for complete replication.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized and presents a logical flow from introduction through methods and experiments, with the two main contributions stated early (phonetic-level LM and constrained generation approach). Notation and hyperparameter reporting appear adequate for reproducibility, and figures/tables/examples seem informative though the summary suggests a few sections (e.g., transliteration/pronunciation pipeline and error analysis) could be clearer. Writing quality is generally good, with only minor clarity issues around limitations and dataset preprocessing that would benefit from slightly more detail. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized with a clear intro, methods, experiments and conclusion, and the two main contributions are stated early (phonetic-level LM and constrained generation with WFSM). Notation and hyperparameter reporting appear consistent, and related work coverage is adequate, but some technical preprocessing steps (transliteration, letter-to-sound rules, HMM/ngram transliteration) and error sources are not fully elucidated in the summary and could benefit from clearer exposition and more informative figures/examples. Writing quality is generally adequate though a few sections explaining phonetic encoding and decoding trade-offs/readability could be tightened for non-specialist readers.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and presents a logical flow from introduction through methods and experiments, with the two main contributions stated early (phonetic-level LM and constrained generation approach). Notation and hyperparameter reporting appear adequate for reproducibility, and figures/tables/examples seem informative though the summary suggests a few sections (e.g., transliteration/pronunciation pipeline and error analysis) could be clearer. Writing quality is generally good, with only minor clarity issues around limitations and dataset preprocessing that would benefit from slightly more detail.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper is well organized with a clear intro, methods, experiments and conclusion, and the two main contributions are stated early (phonetic-level LM and constrained generation with WFSM). Notation and hyperparameter reporting appear consistent, and related work coverage is adequate, but some technical preprocessing steps (transliteration, letter-to-sound rules, HMM/ngram transliteration) and error sources are not fully elucidated in the summary and could benefit from clearer exposition and more informative figures/examples. Writing quality is generally adequate though a few sections explaining phonetic encoding and decoding trade-offs/readability could be tightened for non-specialist readers.

main_claims: Propose two novel methodologies for automatic generation of rhythmic poetry: (1) a neural language model trained on a phonetic encoding that learns form and content implicitly, and (2) a constrained generation approach combining a generative neural language model for content with a discriminative weighted finite-state machine to enforce form.
The phonetic-level model can effectively learn poetic devices such as rhyme, rhythm and alliteration.

The constrained character-level model can generate coherent poetry in arbitrary forms and themes by manipulating constraints, and produced the most formulaic poetry in intrinsic evaluation (70% of lines had zero syllabic-stress mistakes).

A large-scale extrinsic (human) evaluation showed participants considered machine-generated poems to be written by humans 54% of the time, and a machine-generated poem was rated best among evaluated poems.
_summary.methodology: Train an LSTM-based neural language model on a phonetic encoding of poetry (using CMU pronunciation dictionary plus letter-to-sound rules and transliteration via an HMM/ngram model) to learn form and content; alternatively, use a character-level generative neural language model for content combined with a discriminative weighted finite-state machine to impose rhythmic/rhyming constraints.
metrics: percentage of lines with zero syllabic-stress mistakes (Iambic Pentameter conformity)
number of syllabic misclassifications per line
human judgment: percent of machine poems judged as human
limitations: Significant number of errors in the phonetic-level model are likely caused by transliteration mistakes (phonetic encoding and orthographic decoding).
Pronunciation dictionaries suffer from limitations for constructing a syllabic-stress classifier.
Low per-word reconstruction accuracy on Wikipedia due to high frequency of out-of-vocabulary words.
Authors note future work should investigate morpheme-based models, implying current character-based approaches may struggle with complex and rare words common in poetry.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - poetry generation / neural language models

reviewer_a.name: Methodical Engineer
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with 8+ years building and evaluating neural language models for sequence generation in industry and academia; experienced in reproducible experiments, ablation studies, and production concerns.
traits: checks for clear baselines, ablations, and statistically supported comparisons
looks for precise dataset descriptions, preprocessing steps, and hyperparameter choices enabling reproducibility
examines evaluation methodology for bias (e.g., annotation protocols, inter-annotator agreement, statistical tests)
values clarity about failure modes, runtime/memory costs, and implementation trade-offs
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on experimental rigor and reproducibility: will accept claims only when supported by complete comparisons, ablations, and transparent experimental details.

reviewer_b.name: Literary NLP Scholar
reviewer_b.background: Associate professor specializing in computational creativity and NLP for literary forms, with a track record of work on poetic structure, evaluation of creative language systems, and human-centered assessments.
traits: assesses novelty relative to prior computational poetry and constrained generation literature
values the meaningfulness of qualitative analyses and human evaluations in gauging creative quality
notices how linguistic/phonetic preprocessing interacts with poetic devices and whether claims about capturing 'form' are compelling
considers broader impact: generality of methods to other poetic traditions, ethical concerns around authorship, and usefulness to humanities researchers
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Evaluates novelty, interpretability, and real-world significance, weighing both quantitative results and qualitative/human-centered evidence to judge contribution to the field.
